paper_title: Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition?

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within a fixed canvas. As a result, flexible post-generation editing remains challenging, revealing a clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct a data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is a state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as a scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as a practical foundation for layered design editing systems.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 4 1 ] . [ 1 7 6 1 5 1 . 5 0 6 2 : r Does Synthetic Layered Design Data Benefit Layered Design Decomposition? Kam Man Wu1 Haolin Yang1 Qingyu Chen2 Yihu Tang2 Jingye Chen1 Qifeng Chen1 1HKUST 2Webank

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Recent advances in image generation have made it easy to produce high-quality images. However, these outputs are inherently flattened, entangling foreground elements, background, and text within fixed canvas. As result, flexible postgeneration editing remains challenging, revealing clear last-mile gap toward practical usability. Existing approaches either rely on scarce proprietary layered assets or construct partially synthetic data from limited structural priors. However, both strategies face fundamental challenges in scalability. In this work, we investigate whether pure synthetic layered data can improve graphic design decomposition. We make the simplifying assumption that, in graphic design, effective decomposition does not require modeling inter-layer dependencies as precisely as in natural-image composition, since design elements are often intentionally arranged as modular and semantically separable components. Concretely, we conduct data-centric study based on CLD baseline, which is state-of-the-art layer decomposition framework. Based on the baseline, we construct our own synthetic dataset, SynLayers, generate textual supervision using vision language models, and automate inference inputs with VLM-predicted bounding boxes. Our study reveals three key findings: (1) even training with purely synthetic data can outperform non-scalable alternatives such as the widely used PrismLayersPro dataset, demonstrating its viability as scalable and effective substitute; (2) performance consistently improves with increased training data scale, while gains begin to saturate at around 50K samples; and (3) synthetic data enables balanced control over layer-count distributions, avoiding the severe layer-count imbalance commonly observed in real-world datasets. We hope this data-centric study encourages broader adoption of synthetic data as practical foundation for layered design editing systems. We make the code publicly available at GitHub.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Graphic design plays central role in modern content creation, including advertising, social media campaigns, presentations, and digital publishing. As visual generative models continue to mature, they have drawn increasing attention to the last mile of practical editing and controllability [Zhang et al., 2025b, Wei et al., 2025, Zhang et al., 2025a]. In practice, designers compose content in layers using tools such as Adobe Photoshop or PowerPoint, but the exported result is typically single raster image in which foreground elements, background, and text are flattened together [Suzuki et al., 2025]. Consequently, subsequent edits often require manual effort, while generative editing [Hertz et al., 2022, Meng et al., 2021, Jia et al., 2024, Brack et al., 2024] can be unstable and may fail Equal contribution. Project lead. Corresponding authors ((cid:0) jchenha@connect.ust.hk (cid:0) cqf@ust.hk). Preprint. Figure 1: Comparison between fully synthetic graphic-design data from SynLayers and non/partially-synthetic counterparts. In this paper, we explore whether synthetic layer data can help train effective layer decomposition models. We show only four layers from the decomposition results due to limited space. to preserve the geometry and semantics of unchanged regions, even for simple operations such as background flipping or text resizing [Brooks et al., 2023, Couairon et al., 2022, Simsar et al., 2025]. These limitations motivate graphic design decomposition, which aims to recover editable RGBA layers from raster images for reliable and fine-grained control. In this case, graphic design decomposition can be viewed as the last mile that bridges image generation and user-editable outputs, and has recently attracted growing attention from the research community [Suzuki et al., 2025, Yin et al., 2025, Nie et al., 2025]. Existing approaches typically rely on limited amounts of real, non-synthetic graphic design data (e.g., Crello with around 20K samples) or on partially synthetic datasets such as PrismLayersPro with 20K samples, in which layout boxes are derived from real designs. At the same time, visual assets are generated by image models. However, these dataset sizes remain orders of magnitude smaller than those commonly used in modern generative modelingfor instance, text-to-image models are often trained on billions of samples (e.g., LAION-5B [Schuhmann et al., 2022]). This contradicts established scaling laws [Kaplan et al., 2020, Hoffmann et al., 2022], and models trained on limited data often lack robustness and generalization. Therefore, promising alternative is to leverage fully synthetic data, an approach that has demonstrated effectiveness across many domains [Fan et al., 2024, Man and Chahl, 2022]. Yet its potential for layer decomposition remains largely unexplored. In this work, we aim to fill this gap by investigating whether purely synthetic layer data can effectively benefit graphic design decomposition. To answer this question, we propose SynLayers. We carefully design synthetic data pipeline in which graphic design layers are composed from multiple sources, and conduct comprehensive datacentric study. Building on the representative state-of-the-art layer decomposition method, CLD [Liu et al., 2025c], we systematically investigate the role of synthetic data in this task. Specifically, we design layer composition rules to construct diverse synthetic samples. Figure 1 shows the advantages of SynLayers. We further fine-tune VLM to generate high-quality captions for our constructed synthetic data, providing additional semantic supervision for layer decomposition. Through extensive experiments, our data-centric study aims to answer the following questions: Can synthetic data serve as viable alternative to non-/partially synthetic datasets? Yes. With the same amount of training data (18K samples), synthetic data achieves comparable or better performance on most metrics, with improvements in both layer PSNR (+1.01) and composite PSNR (+0.83), indicating stronger reconstruction quality at both the layer and image levels. Does synthetic data follow scaling laws, i.e., does performance improve consistently with increased data size? No. Performance does not improve monotonically with more synthetic data. For example, the best results are achieved at medium scales, with Layer FID reaching 5.97 at 20K and Composite FID reaching 10.35 at 30K. Does synthetic data improve training stability regarding distribution balance? Yes. Synthetic data yields more robust performance across different layer counts. It consistently outperforms the baseline across different layer-count settings, with more gains in challenging cases. For example, when the number of layers is between 13 and 35, Mask IoU improves from 0.901 to 0.910, and Composite PSNR improves from 29.48 to 30.25. 2 Table 1: Comparison with existing multi-layer graphic design datasets. Our work fills the gap by investigating purely synthetic data. SynLayers can scale to much larger datasets, and we use 500K samples for analysis.

sections.title: Yes
sections.content: We hope this study demonstrates that synthetic data is practical and scalable option for training layer-decomposition models and encourages its use in building more reliable and controllable graphic design editing systems.

sections.title: 2 Related Works
sections.content: 2.1 Layered Image Decomposition Layered image decomposition separates raster images into editable RGBA layers, enabling flexible reconstruction and modification. Several research works Liu et al. [2025b,a], Lungu-Stan et al. [2026], Kang et al. [2025], Huang et al. [2025], Zhang et al. [2023], Zhang and Agrawala [2024], Dalva et al. [2024], Huang et al. [2024] focus on this problem. For example, LayerD [Suzuki et al., 2025] employs iterative matting and inpainting but offers limited user control, while Accordion [Chen et al., 2025a] utilizes VLMs and SAM [Kirillov et al., 2023] in top-down pipeline to recover layers from flattened AI-generated designs. Bottom-up and layout-aware approaches include COLE [Jia et al., 2023] and Open-COLE [Inoue et al., 2024], which cascade LLMs with diffusion models, and Composition-Aware Graphic LayoutGAN [Zhou et al., 2022], which learns structural priors for visualtextual arrangements. Recently, diffusion-based models such as Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025] have introduced variable-length disentanglement via RGBA-VAE and VLD-MMDiT. However, it suffers from high inference latency due to the heavy diffusion design. Notably, CLD [Liu et al., 2025c], which is built on top of ART Pu et al. [2025], introduces LD-DiT and MLCA components for bounding-box-guided decomposition, offering an efficient alternative to heavier diffusion design. Given the trade-off between controllability and computational efficiency, we adopt CLD as our basic framework. 2.2 Datasets for Multi-Layer Graphic Design Decomposition Multi-layer datasets are limited because clean RGBA layers are usually stored in proprietary design files rather than in publicly available raster image datasets. Existing resources such as MuLan [Tudosiu et al., 2024] and MAGICK [Burgert et al., 2024] provide some layer annotations or simple composites, but they lack the complexity and rich text content needed for graphic design tasks. Within the graphic design domain, existing resources are constrained by scale and synthesis depth. CanvasVAE [Yamaguchi, 2021] releases layered graphic dataset, Crello, while PrismLayersPro [Chen et al., 2025b] provides high-quality dataset of 20K samples with transparent layers. Recently, LICA [Hirsch et al., 2026] introduced layered image composition annotation strategy to support graphic design research. As shown in Table 1, we present clear comparison between SynLayers and existing alternatives to explore the potential of synthetic data. 2.3 Synthetic Data for Vision Tasks Synthetic data has become cornerstone of modern computer vision pipelines, particularly for mitigating data scarcity [Delussu et al., 2024, Mumuni et al., 2024, Nikolenko et al., 2021, Jordon et al., 2022, Westerski and Fong, 2024, Nowruzi et al., 2019, Vanherle et al., 2022]. The limitations of existing layered design datasets reflect broader challenges where curated real-world supervision is expensive or difficult to obtain. In such scenarios, synthetic data generation has proven effective across diverse domains. For example, in OCR tasks, synthetic text generation has long been employed to supplement training data, with early works utilizing millions of synthetic samples to improve recognition robustness [Jaderberg et al., 2014]. In the generative era, models like Qwen-Image utilize synthetic visuals, including slides and banners, to address the lack of text-rich real-world training 3 Figure 2: Overview of construction of SynLayers. Multi-source assets, including base designs, RGBA/RGB foregrounds, and text layers, are recombined with low-overlap algorithm to generate composite images, spatial bounding boxes, and raw layout descriptions. VLM refines these into coherent whole-image captions. The output is fully synthetic layered dataset comprising composite images, ground-truth layer boxes, and structured captions, which provides complete supervision for decomposition training. data [Wu et al., 2025]. Similarly, studies have demonstrated that synthetic images can achieve parity with real data in recognition tasks [He et al., 2022], while in 3D vision, synthetic assets facilitate training reconstruction models such as LRM-Zero [Xie et al., 2024]. Inspired by these successes, we utilize SynLayers to investigate the effectiveness of synthetic data for graphic design decomposition.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: This section is organized as follows. Section 3.1 introduces CLD as the preliminary knowledge. Section 3.2 describes the strategy of processing multi-source data and caption construction. Section 3.3 trains VLM for layout detection for automatic decomposition. In Figure 2 We illustrate the construction procedure of SynLayers. 3.1 Preliminary Knowledge of CLD Our work uses CLD as the base decomposition architecture, which we describe in detail here. Given raster image RHW 3, the objective is to generate set of RGBA layers = {D0, D1, . . . , DN 1}, where D0 denotes the composited image, D1 represents the background layer, and D2 to DN 1 correspond to the foreground alpha layers. Following CLD, the decomposition is conditioned on whole-image caption Ctext and set of bounding boxes = {Bcomp, B0, B1, . . . , BN 1}. Here, Bcomp and B0 correspond to the composite and background images, both of which span the full image region with coordinates [0, 0, W, H], while the remaining boxes B1, . . . , BN 1 denote foreground regions. For any box Bi = (xl ), each coordinate is adjusted to be divisible by 16. , yr i, xr i, yl CLD adopts crop-then-denoise pipeline. Instead of processing the entire image, it first crops features from each layers bounding box and then performs joint denoising across all cropped regions. This design restricts computation to the spatial extent of valid layers, making the model more efficient and more scalable to varying numbers of layers than Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025]. Finally, transparent decoder maps the denoised representations back to RGBA layers. 3.2 Multi-Source Synthetic Dataset Construction High-quality graphic design data with per-layer RGBA supervision remains scarce. To address this limitation, we construct SynLayers, fully synthetic training set generated via multi-source layer-composition pipeline that systematically assembles diverse elements into coherent designs. Figure 3 shows the samples selected from SynLayers. Each sample is generated through the following procedure: Base layout construction. We initialize from reference design, preserving its background and subset of foreground elements to maintain structural consistency. Cross-source layer integration. We sample additional designs and incorporate selected foreground elements, increasing compositional diversity while preserving design plausibility. 4 Figure 3: Our samples illustrate the strength of SynLayers, which are fully synthetic, with high-quality RGBA layers, and ensure the layout diversity. Auxiliary element augmentation. Additional elements are introduced following predefined probabilities: LAION image crops (probability 0.60, relative scale in [0.3, 0.4] of the canvas), Rendered text layers (probability 0.35, relative scale in [0.6, 0.8] of the canvas), AlphaVAE foreground objects (up to 3 instances per sample, relative scale in [0.25, 0.40] of the canvas) [Schuhmann et al., 2022, Wang et al., 2025]. Spatial arrangement. Layer placement is determined via candidate sampling to reduce overlap and ensure spatial coherence. Full details of the generation pipeline, including size configurations, are provided in Appendix A.1. Layer composition and placement. To place each layer, we aim to minimize spatial overlap with existing elements. For layer with size (wj, hj), we sample set of candidate positions Sj and select the position that minimizes the normalized overlap: (x 0, 0) = arg min (x0,y0)Sj 1 Area(Bj) (cid:88) BkBocc Area(Bj Bk), where Bj = [x0, y0, x0 + wj, y0 + hj] denotes the bounding box of the candidate placement, and Bocc is the set of previously placed boxes. If zero-overlap candidate exists, it is selected. Otherwise, the candidate minimizing overlap is chosen. This procedure preserves the aspect ratio of each layer while promoting spatial separation and layout diversity. Caption construction and refinement. We divide the canvas into 3 3 grid, resulting in nine coarse spatial regions (e.g., top-left, top, and bottom-right). Each layer is assigned to one of these regions based on the center of its bounding box. We construct an initial caption by traversing the grid in reading order and describing the content in each region. For each layer, we combine its spatial location with source-specific description, which may come from PrismLayersPro metadata, LAION image-text pairs, rendered text layers, or AlphaVAE layers. The resulting caption consists of spatial phrases paired with corresponding content descriptions. To improve fluency and coherence, we further refine this draft using VLM conditioned on the composite image. The final caption is used as text supervision for both CLD training and the downstream detector (see Appendix A.2). 3.3 VLM-Guided Automated Input Generation CLD requires foreground boxes and global caption as inputs during inference. Instead of relying on dataset annotations as in prior work [Liu et al., 2025c], we train single VLM to jointly predict both from the raster image in one forward pass, i.e., fψ : (cid:55) ( ˆCtext, ˆBu). We build the training data from the SynLayers by pairing each image with its caption and layer boxes, and formatting them as instruction-following training samples. We then fine-tune Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct [Bai et al., 2025] with LoRA [Hu et al., 2022] to predict both captions and bounding boxes. At inference time, the VLM outputs JSON object containing the image caption and predicted boxes. We further add the composite and background boxes, and quantize all boxes to the 16-pixel grid required by CLD. See Appendix A.3 and A.4 for details. This creates pipeline where the structured outputs from the upstream model directly condition the downstream model [Yang et al., 2025]. Figure 4: Qualitative comparison between the original PrismLayersPro-trained CLD baseline, our SynLayers-trained model, Qwen-Image-Layered, and the ground-truth decomposition. Across examples, the SynLayers-trained model produces sharper typography, cleaner object boundaries, and layer layouts that more closely match the target decomposition.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Results
sections.content: All models are fine-tuned from FLUX.1[dev] [Black Forest Labs, 2024] using LoRA with rank 64 on LD-DiT and MLCA. We follow CLD [Liu et al., 2025c] and keep the denoising objective unchanged. To ensure fair comparison, all models use the same training settings, so performance differences primarily reflect the impact of the training data. Training details are provided in Appendix A.5. Detailed comparison between the ground truth and SynLayers output is in Appendix A.6. 4.1 Qualitative Results Figure 4 presents qualitative comparisons between methods. Both CLD-based models (trained in SynLayers or PrismLayersPro) substantially outperform Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025] in the testset, which produces misaligned decompositions with natural layer structures. This stems from Qwen-Image-Layereds requirement for predefined layer count and its tendency to over-segment semantic elements into fragmented parts. Even when instructed with the correct layer count, it fails to achieve meaningful decomposition, highlighting the advantage of our VLM-detector pipeline, which automatically predicts layer counts and semantic descriptions. Comparing our SynLayers-trained model with the PrismLayersPro baseline reveals clear quality improvements. Our model produces sharper object boundaries with fewer visual artifacts (Fig. 4, row 1, col. 2 vs. 4), such as fewer blue patches inside letter e. Text segmentation is enhanced: the Merry Christmas text layer (row 2) shows cleaner character separation, and small fonts such as We Hope You Have exhibit sharper character appearance and clearer visual effect compared to baseline. These improvements arise from the diverse caption layers in our SynLayers, which better prepare the model for text decomposition. In real-world samples, as Figure 5 shows, SynLayers shows better performance than the PrismLayersPro baseline and Qwen-Image-Layered. For example, when evaluating the fourth row, our SynLayers-trained model achieves the best visual clarity when decomposing the human head layer compared to Qwen-Image-Layered and PrismLayersPro baseline. Overall, SynLayers-trained model achieves cleaner visual effects, more accurate object boundaries, and layer layouts that more closely match ground-truth decompositions, demonstrating the effectiveness of SynLayers. 4.2 Quantitative Results Table 2 quantifies how the training scale affects the quality of the decomposition. In comparison of the same dataset size between PrismLayersPro of 18K samples and SynLayers of 18k samples. SynLayers-trained model outperforms the PrismLayersPro baseline across key metrics. For example, Layer PSNR from 26.22 to 27.23 and Composite SSIM from 0.944 to 0.950. This result confirms that purely synthetic layers are viable and data-efficient substitute. Our 1k to 500k dataset size scaling analysis reveals that the training effect gains peak at medium sizes: Layer FID reaches its minimum 5.97 at 20K, while Composite FID improves to 10.35 at 30K. Beyond this range, 6 Figure 5: Qualitative comparison on the out-of-distribution real-world dataset. From left to right: Ground Truth composite image, the original CLD baseline, our SynLayers-trained model, and Qwen-Image-Layered. Driven entirely by the Qwen3-VL detectors output, our method yields cleaner semantic separations, sharper details, and higher composite fidelity compared to the baseline and external references. Table 2: Quantitative comparison across training-set scales. The table includes the PrismLayersPro reference and matched-size SynLayers 18K subset, enabling direct comparison at the same nominal data budget. Table 3 provides direct reference comparison to Qwen-Image-Layered and the PrismLayersPro baseline. Qwen-Image-Layereds poor layer-wise scores, like PSNR 13.80 and FID 198.34, are due to its requirement for fixed layer count and tendency to over-segment semantic elements, which misaligns with open-ended editing needs. Although composite reconstruction offers fairer metric by mitigating fragmentation artifacts, Qwen-Image-Layered still lags behind both CLD and our model. Our 20K checkpoint consistently improves layer-wise and composite fidelity over the baseline. For example, decreasing Layer FID from 6.62 to 5.97 and Composite FID from 12.50 to 12.00, confirming that synthetic training preserves CLDs decomposition behavior while enhancing reconstruction quality and practical editability. 7 Table 3: Quantitative comparison between Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025], the original PrismLayersPro trained CLD baseline, and our best medium-scale SynLayers-trained checkpoint. Model PSNR Layer-wise SSIM FID PSNR Composite SSIM Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025] PrismLayersPro SynLayers (Ours) 13.80 26.22 27.16 0.509 0.865 0.880 198.34 6.62 5.97 27.19 30.52 30.82 0.899 0.944 0.948 FID 33.23 12.50 12.00 Table 4: Composite-only evaluation on our 147-image out-of-distribution real-world testset. For the CLD-based methods, inference inputs are produced automatically by our trained Qwen3-VL-based detector, which predicts the whole-image caption and layer bounding boxes for each image. Model PSNR SSIM FID Qwen-Image-Layered [Yin et al., 2025] PrismLayersPro SynLayers (Ours) 28.56 28.74 29.35 0.843 0.852 0.856 64.30 44.23 35.40 We further assess the generalization ability among different models on 147-image out-of-distribution (OOD) real-world test set, where inference is fully automated by our fine-tuned Qwen3-VL detector, which can predict image-level captions and layer bounding boxes. Detailed detector evaluations are in Appendix A.7. As layer-level ground truth is unavailable, we report composite reconstruction metrics in Table 4. Our SynLayers-trained checkpoint consistently outperforms the original baseline CLD, such as PSNR from 28.74 to 29.35 and FID from 44.23 to 35.40, demonstrating robust transfer to diverse unseen layouts. In contrast, Qwen-Image-Layed [Yin et al., 2025] exhibits sharp increase in FID to 64.30, reflecting weaker distribution-level realism under OOD conditions. These results provide preliminary evidence that SynLayers can achieve superior performance in real-world layerdecomposition tasks. Revealing the potential to act as the foundational method of constructing dataset in image editing and layer design domains. 4.3 Discussion Training dynamics of the 500K model. Figure 6 tracks evaluation metrics across checkpoints from 10K to 500K. Training exhibits rapid early gains followed by an oscillatory plateau. Using Composite PSNR and FID as indicators, reconstruction quality improves sharply from 29.24 to 30.89 from 10K to 40K, then stabilizes. Conversely, distributional realism peaks at 40K with an FID of 12.45, degrades to 18.25 by 50K, and subsequently fluctuates in the 20-22 range. This establishes the 40K-60K window as the optimal trade-off, balancing pixel fidelity and distributional alignment. Extending training to 500K maintains reconstruction quality but yields no further gains. PSNR SSIM Mask Metrics FID 28 26 0.95 0.9 0.85 0.96 0.94 0.92 0.9 0. 1050 100 200 300 450 1050 100 200 300 450 1050 100 200 300 450 40 30 20 10 0 1050 200 300 450 500 Layer PSNR Layer FID Composite PSNR Composite FID Layer SSIM Mask IoU Composite SSIM Mask F1 Figure 6: Checkpoint-level training dynamics of the full SynLayers-trained model. All curves use the recorded evaluation values at each retained checkpoint from 10K to 500K. Performance by layer count. key advantage of SynLayers is the ability to construct training samples with arbitrary layer counts. The PrismLayersPro training set is constrained by the layer structure or layout design of real design files, which limits the diversity of layer counts during training. In contrast, SynLayers flexibly comprises varying number of layers using multi-source images. Higher values are better for PSNR and SSIM, while lower values are better for FID. (c) Mask-quality metrics across layer-count bins. Higher values are better Original SynLayers 18K SynLayers 30K SynLayers 50K SynLayers 100K SynLayers 500K Figure 7: Left column reports layer-wise and composite metrics, and the right reports mask-quality metrics. Table 5: Layer-count distribution across the PrismLayersPro and SynLayers of different scales. Layer bin / summary Original (18K) SynLayers 18K SynLayers 30K SynLayers 50K SynLayers 100K SynLayers 500K 1 to 5 6 to 10 11 to 15 16 to 20 21 to 25 26 to 52 6 to 15 share 1 to 20 share 3,367 8,923 4,376 959 249 126 73.9% 97.9% 1,407 6,331 6,809 2,615 594 73.0% 95.3% 2,304 10,738 11,342 4,182 1,034 400 73.6% 95.2% 3,896 17,841 18,867 7,015 1,695 686 73.4% 95.2% 7,769 35,483 37,597 14,391 3,385 1, 73.1% 95.2% 38,993 178,181 187,444 71,604 16,899 6,879 73.1% 95.2% the PrismLayersPro test set into four contiguous ground-truth layer-count bins chosen to be as even as possible over the 1 to 35 layer range: 1 to 7, 8 to 9, 10 to 12, and 13 to 35 layers. Each bin contains 300, 265, 247, and 187 samples, respectively. As shown in Figure 7, Layer-reconstruction metrics (Fig. 7a) consistently favor training using SynLayers, with the 18K SynLayers subset trained checkpoint leading in pixelwise quality and Layer FID improving across all bins. Composite results (Fig. 7b) are more heterogeneous: 18K achieves the highest PSNR, while 30K yields the lowest FID, reinforcing our core trade-off between reconstruction fidelity and distributional realism. Mask quality (Fig. 7c) also consistently benefits from synthetic supervision, with 18K50K checkpoints outperforming the baseline in IoU and F1 across all complexity levels. Overall, SynLayers can reliably enhance editable structure and mask accuracy, with performance stabilizing at moderate scales. Overall, this balanced-bin evaluation indicates that the benefit of synthetic layered design data is not confined to narrow layer-count band. SynLayers-trained models improve most decompositionoriented metrics across all four bins, while Table 5 shows that the training sample distribution of SynLayers remains concentrated in moderate-complexity samples rather than becoming uniform over all layer counts.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this paper, we propose SynLayers, synthetic layered designed dataset. Our work demonstrates that synthetic layered designed data provides practical and scalable foundation for graphic design 9 decomposition. By integrating VLM-based detector with the CLD backbone, we automate inference inputs and validate that synthetic data supervision consistently enhances layer reconstruction quality and Composite image visual effect. Future work will focus on improving the grounding for irregular elements, incorporating professional blending effects, disentangling supervision from conditioning, and evaluating decomposed outputs in interactive workflows. We hope that this study encourages the broader adoption of synthetic data as scalable foundation for controllable design systems.

sections.title: A Supplementary Material
sections.content: A.1 Additional Methodology Details Xinput = (cid:2)ˆzcrop Inherited CLD backbone. We keep the CLD architecture [Liu et al., 2025c] unchanged, modifying only the supervision and conditioning pipeline. LD-DiT processes multi-layer latents by cropping each layer to its quantized bounding box Bq , flattening the tokens, and concatenating them into single sequence: = Flatten(Crop(ˆzi, Bq (cid:3) , where ˆzcrop )). This factorization restricts computation to valid layer supports, enabling efficient handling of variable layer counts. MLCA injects image-conditioned residuals through zero-initialized adapter projections: h(k) block h(k) block + λ Ak with conditioning scale λ = 0.9. transparent VAE decoder reconstructs RGBA outputs by scattering decoded tokens back to their spatial supports. During inference, we retain CLDs dual-condition classifier-free guidance: ; . . . ; ˆzcrop 1 comp; ˆzcrop h(k) MLCA (cid:16) (cid:17) , ˆv = vθ(xt, t, , I) + [vθ(xt, t, Ctext, I) vθ(xt, t, , I)] , where the image is preserved in both branches as structural anchor and only the text condition is dropped in the unconditional branch (s = 4.0). All backbone weights are frozen; we fine-tune only LoRA adapters (rank 64) injected into LD-DiT and MLCA. Dataset construction pipeline. Our synthetic data generation pipeline operates on 1024 1024 RGBA canvases and follows multi-stage composition strategy: 1. Base sample selection: We sample from the first 18K PrismLayersPro-blended training samples (excluding the held-out test set). For each base, we retain the background canvas and randomly remove Nremove U{1, 4} foreground layers while ensuring at least one remains. 2. Donor layer sampling: We sample Ndonors U{1, 4} distinct donor designs and extract Nlayers U{0, 2} foreground layers from each. Donor layers preserve their original RGBA crops and bounding box dimensions. 3. Auxiliary content insertion (probabilistic): LAION crops: Added with probability pLAION = 0.60, sized at [0.3, 0.4] canvas dimensions. Rendered text layers: Added with probability ptext = 0.35, sized at [0.6, 0.8] canvas; bounding boxes are tightened to non-transparent content via α-channel analysis. AlphaVAE foreground objects: NAlphaVAE U{0, 3} objects, sized at [0.25, 0.40] canvas, using generation prompts as captions. 4. Overlap-minimizing placement: For each candidate layer with dimensions (wj, hj), we sample up to 300 random placements and select the one minimizing normalized overlap with occupied boxes: (x 0, 0) = arg min (x0,y0)Sj 1 Area(Bj) (cid:88) BkBocc Area(Bj Bk), where zero-overlap candidates are accepted immediately. 5. Metadata serialization: Each sample is saved with layer-wise metadata (source type, bounding box, caption, image path) and composite image. Caption construction and refinement. Raw captions are built by traversing 3 3 spatial grid in reading order and concatenating position-tagged layer descriptions (source-specific: PrismLayersPro metadata, LAION pairings, rendered text, or AlphaVAE prompts). This grid-based draft is refined by Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct, conditioned on both the composite image and raw caption, using system prompt that requests: (1) 100 to 140 word output, (2) first 40 words as holistic overview, (3) remaining 60 to 100 words for layer-level details, overlaps, and visible English text. More detailed illustration will be in Figure 8 13 VLM training and inference serialization. For auto-detector training, each synthesized sample is serialized as an instruction-following pair requesting JSON object with keys whole_caption and boxes. We fine-tune Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct with LoRA (rank 8, lora_target=all), cutoff length 3072, per-device batch size 4, gradient accumulation 4, learning rate 1.5 104, cosine scheduling, warmup ratio 0.1, and 3 epochs in bf16. At inference, predicted JSON outputs will be all combined into jsonl file, whose format can be consumed by the CLD pipeline. Implementation details. The pipeline is implemented in Python with multiprocessing (64 workers) for scalability. Random seeds are fixed per sample for reproducibility. LAION images are capped at 20K for balanced diversity. All generated samples (500K total) are indexed in jsonl file with deterministic ordering by sample ID. A.2 Caption Refinement Process Illustration Caption Refinement from Raw Grid-Based Description to Coherent Text Input composite image: Raw grid-based caption (before refinement): The image youve provided is solid, uniform color with no distinguishable features or objects. In the center, This is doodle_art style image. The picture shows blank white square with green border. On the top-right, This is doodle art style image. sleek bicycle is depicted with intricate line work, showcasing its frame, handlebars, and two large wheels ...... On the left, pretzel is perfectly centered in the frame, minimalist composition, on soft pink pastel background. System prompt for refinement: You are an expert image captioner. Your task is to refine and condense long, redundant whole caption of layered image. Requirements: (1) Conciseness: Keep the final caption between 100 to 140 words! (2) Natural Flow: Blend the background and layers into cohesive, professional paragraph. Avoid repetitive phrases like you can see or there is. (3) Output Format: Return ONLY the refined caption string. (4) Accuracy and Vividness: Ensure descriptions precisely match visual elements, using vivid but concise language. (5) First 40 words should provide an overview; remaining 60 to 100 words detail layer-level descriptions. (6) Describe overlapped layers concisely. (7) For English text layers, describe the text content in detail. Refined caption (after Qwen2.5-VL processing): whimsical doodle art-style image features sleek bicycle with intricate line work, large teal circle with playful edge, bold distressed letters spelling "RIMBERIO," and charming heart and bubbles. The bikes minimalist yet elegant design contrasts with the playful, hand-drawn elements surrounding it. 14 Figure 8: Illustration of the caption refinement process. The raw grid-based caption (constructed by traversing the 3 3 grid and describing each layer with position tags) is refined by Qwen2.5-VL-3B-Instruct into coherent, natural-language description. The refined caption serves as ground-truth text supervision for training both the CLD decomposition model and the Qwen3-VL detector. A.3 Illustration of VLM Supervision and Inference Figure 10 illustrates the supervision format used to fine-tune the Qwen3-VL detector and representative post-training inference output. A.4 Training Details for detector The detector, responsible for generating global captions and spatial layout constraints (bounding boxes), was trained via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on SynLayers, our spatially-annotated synthetic dataset. We fine-tuned the Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct model [Bai et al., 2025] using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) within the Llama Factory framework. To ensure high-resolution spatial awareness, the input images were processed at resolution of 1024 1024 pixels (1,048,576 total pixels). The model was trained on 4 NVIDIA H20 GPUs. The detailed SFT hyperparameters are summarized in Table 6. Table 6: Hyperparameters for detector LoRA Fine-tuning. Parameter Value Base Model Fine-tuning Method LoRA Target Modules Epochs Learning Rate Scheduler Initial Learning Rate Total Batch Size Number of GPUs Per-device Batch Size Gradient Accumulation Max Sequence Length Precision Attention Mechanism Image Resolution Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct LoRA (Rank=8, Alpha=16) All linear layers 3 Cosine 1.5 104 64 4 (NVIDIA H20) 4 4 steps 3072 bf16 Flash-Attention 2 1024 A.5 Training Details for the Decomposition Model Our decomposition model is fine-tuned from FLUX.1[dev] [Black Forest Labs, 2024]. We initialize the MultiLayer-Adapter from the released CLD adapter checkpoint and load the released CLD LoRA weights before injecting new LoRA modules into both LD-DiT and MLCA. During training, all backbone weights remain frozen except the LoRA parameters and the layer-position embeddings. The script supports both single-GPU and multi-GPU execution; in multi-GPU mode, it uses manual gradient all-reduce at accumulation boundaries instead of DDP wrappers to avoid conflicts with gradient checkpointing, and we do the training on 8 H800 GPUs. The detailed hyperparameters are summarized in Table 7. A.6 More comparison examples between Ground Truth and SynLayers In this section, we will present cases in Figure 9 and compare the inference output from our SynLayerstrained model with the actual ground truth. A.7 Quantitative Evaluation of the Qwen3-VL detector To rigorously assess the reliability of the predicted spatial constraints, Table 8 reports both strict objectdiscovery metrics and matched-box localization quality for the detector. Under strict evaluation protocol that explicitly penalizes both missed and hallucinated bounding boxes, the detector achieves 91.26% precision, 82.34% recall, and 86.57% F1 at IoU= 0.50, corresponding to 1525 true positives, 146 false positives, and 327 false negatives. Under standard detection-style evaluation, it further obtains AP@0.50 of 76.58%, AP@0.75 of 70.16%, and mAP@[0.50:0.95] of 68.02%. These results 15 Figure 9: More comparison examples between Ground Truth and SynLayers 16 Table 7: Hyperparameters for decomposition-model fine-tuning. Parameter Value FLUX.1[dev] FLUX.1-dev-Controlnet-Inpainting-Alpha Base Model Adapter Initialization Pretrained LoRA Initialization Released CLD LoRA weights LoRA on LD-DiT and MLCA Fine-tuning Method LoRA weights + layer PE Trainable Parameters 64 / 64 / 0 LoRA Rank / Alpha / Dropout Prodigy Optimizer 1.0 Nominal Learning Rate (0.9, 0.999) Betas 0.001 Weight Decay Constant (LambdaLR) Learning Rate Scheduler Masked flow-matching MSE Training Objective 1 Per-device Batch Size 4 steps Gradient Accumulation 4 number of GPUs Effective Batch Size Precision bf16 Enabled for LD-DiT and MLCA Gradient Checkpointing 512 Max Text Sequence Length 4.0 Classifier-free Guidance Scale 0.9 MLCA Conditioning Scale 28 Inference Denoising Steps 52 Maximum Layer Number 42 Random Seed provide substantially more realistic assessment of the detector in fully automated pipeline than matched-box metrics alone, showing that object discovery is strong but still imperfect. Illustration of VLM Supervision and Inference for Automated Input Generation Training sample. Input instruction (simplified): <image> This image is 1024 pixels in width and 1024 pixels in height. First describe the whole image in one detailed caption (whole_caption). Then list the bounding box for each visible layer or object in the image. Each box is in the format [x0, y0, x1, Output single JSON object with y1]. exactly two keys: whole_caption and boxes. other text or markdown. Output only this JSON, no Target output: {"whole_caption": "...", "boxes": [[102,212,921,825], [234,387,789,729], ...]} Synthetic training image Inference result after LoRA fine-tuning. 17 Input instruction (simplified): <image> This image is 1024 pixels in width and 1024 pixels in height. First describe the whole image in one detailed caption (whole_caption). Then list the bounding box for each visible layer or object in the image. Each box is in the format [x0, y0, x1, Output single JSON object with y1]. exactly two keys: whole_caption and boxes. other text or markdown. Output only this JSON, no welcoming guests into cozy Predicted output: {"whole_caption": "The image features vibrant pink background ... space.", "boxes": [[35,262,994,773], [817,484,926,600], [0,624,226,859], [393,257,452,313], [277,387,742,522], [294,505,726,593], [675,566,739,637], [172,282,818,711]]} Post-training prediction with bounding-box visualization Inference flow: 1. Whole-image caption generation. Step summary: The model first generates whole_caption, summarizing the semantic content, visual style, and overall composition of the design. 2. Layer localization. Step summary: The model then predicts sequence of layer boxes that localize the editable elements to be passed to the CLD backbone. 3. Structured serialization. Step summary: Finally, the caption and boxes are organized into machine-readable JSON object and converted into the jsonl format expected by the downstream inference wrapper. Figure 10: Illustration of the supervision format and post-training inference for the Qwen3-VL detector. The upper example shows synthetic training sample paired with structured caption-and-box target, while the lower example shows post-training prediction visualized with bounding boxes. Together, these examples illustrate how the model converts raster image into the structured inputs required by the downstream decomposition pipeline. For localization quality on successfully matched boxes, the detector still achieves matched mIoU of 0.9547 and matched mGIoU of 0.9544. Furthermore, the mean center-distance error is only 3.66 pixels on 1024 1024 canvas (0.0025 in normalized coordinates), indicating that once an object is detected, its spatial extent is estimated with very high geometric precision. Since the VLM detector does not produce calibrated per-box confidence scores, AP/mAP is computed with uniform scores over predicted boxes. In addition to box prediction, we also evaluate the quality of the predicted whole_caption using GPT-4.1 as an LLM judge; the full prompt used for that judging procedure is given at the end of this subsection. As shown in Table 9, the predicted captions achieve an overall score of 80.77/100 on 200 evaluation samples. The model performs particularly well on coverage of salient content (4.340/5), reference alignment (4.280/5), and fluency and structure (4.975/5), indicating that it generally captures the main semantic content of the image and expresses it coherently. The main weakness lies in text accuracy and non-hallucination (3.460/5), suggesting that errors are primarily caused by incorrect transcription of visible text and occasional unsupported details. Overall, these results indicate that the detector provides not only reliable spatial priors for layer decomposition but also semantically strong global captions that can support downstream generation. 18 Table 8: Bounding-box evaluation results for the Qwen3-VL detector on 200-sample test set. Strict object-discovery metrics penalize both missed and hallucinated boxes, whereas matched-box metrics evaluate only the localization quality of successfully matched predictions. Metric Strict object discovery Precision@0.50 Recall@0.50 F1@0.50 Detection-style AP/mAP AP@0.50 AP@0.75 mAP@[0.50:0.95] Matched-box localization quality Matched mIoU Matched mGIoU Center distance (px) Center distance (normalized) Value 91.26% 82.34% 86.57% 76.58% 70.16% 68.02% 0.9547 0.9544 3.66 0.0025 Table 9: GPT-4.1-based evaluation results for predicted whole_caption on the 200-sample test set. The overall score is weighted average computed from five criteria. Metric Judged samples Overall score (0 to 100) Criterion means (1 to 5) Image faithfulness Coverage of salient content Reference alignment Text accuracy and non-hallucination Fluency and structure Verdict counts Excellent Good Mixed Poor Bad Value 200 80.77 3.925 4.340 4.280 3.460 4.975 49 89 53 8 1 LLM Judge Evaluation Prompt. Prompt for GPT-4.1-based Caption Evaluation System Prompt: You are strict but fair multimodal caption judge. You will receive: 1. the image 2. ground-truth caption (reference only, not an exact wording target) 3. predicted caption to evaluate Evaluation policy: Use the image as the primary source of truth. Use the ground-truth caption only as semantic reference. Do not punish harmless paraphrases or different ordering. Penalize hallucinated objects, text, numbers, colors, styles, or relations. Penalize missing major salient content. If the prediction captures the image well but differs stylistically from GT, still score it well. Many images contain rendered text. Incorrect text transcription should be penalized. Return JSON only, with no markdown fences and no extra commentary. User Prompt Template: You are evaluating one predicted whole-image caption. ## Scoring criteria Score each criterion from 1 to 5: 1. image_faithfulness (Weight: 0.35) 5: strongly faithful to visible image content 3: mostly correct but with some noticeable mistakes or omissions 1: largely inconsistent with the image 2. coverage_of_salient_content (Weight: 0.20) 5: covers most important objects, layout regions, and visually dominant text 19 3: covers only part of the key content 1: misses major salient content 3. reference_alignment (Weight: 0.20) 5: semantically consistent with the ground-truth caption 3: partially aligned but misses important GT meaning 1: strongly conflicts with GT semantics 4. text_accuracy_and_non_hallucination (Weight: 0.20) 5: avoids unsupported details and handles visible text well 3: some speculative or inaccurate details 1: obvious hallucinations or badly incorrect text content 5. fluency_and_structure (Weight: 0.05) 5: clear, coherent, well-formed caption 3: understandable but awkward or verbose 1: hard to read or poorly structured ## Important instructions The image is the main evidence. The ground-truth caption is reference, not wording template. If GT itself seems slightly incomplete, do not force the prediction to copy it. Penalize hallucinations more than small wording differences. Focus on semantic correctness, salient visual coverage, and visible text accuracy. ## Ground-truth caption {gt_caption} ## Predicted caption {pred_caption} ## Output format Return single JSON object with this exact schema: { "image_faithfulness": "coverage_of_salient_content": {"score": 1-5, "reason": "short reason"}, {"score": 1-5, "reason": "short reason"}, "reference_alignment": "text_accuracy_and_non_hallucination": {"score": 1-5, "reason": "short reason"}, {"score": 1-5, "reason": "short reason"}, "fluency_and_structure": {"score": 1-5, "reason": "short reason"}, "verdict": "excellentgoodmixedpoorbad", "error_types": ["optional_short_tags"], "summary": "2-4 sentence overall explanation" } A.8 Compute Resources The main decomposition-model training runs were conducted on 4 NVIDIA H800 GPUs. In our training setup, obtaining one trained checkpoint for the main decomposition sweep required roughly 5 days of wall-clock time on these 4 GPUs. The detector training used 4 NVIDIA H20 GPUs, as summarized in Appendix A.4. Beyond the checkpoints reported in the paper, the overall project also required additional exploratory runs and failed tests that are not individually listed in the main experimental tables.

sections.title: B Broader Impacts
sections.content: Our work studies whether synthetic layered design data can improve layered design decomposition, with the positive goal of reducing dependence on scarce proprietary layered assets and making editable-layer research more accessible. If successful, such methods can support practical creative workflows by making rasterized graphic designs easier to edit, analyze, and reuse in educational, research, and design-assistance settings.

sections.title: C Limitations
sections.content: Several limitations remain. The synthetic pipeline has not yet model professional design effects, such as complex blending modes, stylized transparency, or highly irregular text layouts. The VLM-based detector still relies on rectangular boxes and JSON generation, which limits performance on nonrectangular or highly overlapping design elements, and our detector evaluation remains limited to box metrics and whole-caption quality rather than full downstream human-editing studies. The current evaluation also lacks repeated-seed uncertainty estimates and uses only small out-of-distribution real-world set without layer-level ground truth for external validation.
